Also PETG likes to ooze; you often manage it with: - Lower temp within acceptable range, - Faster travel, - Combing/wipe, - Keep filament dry. When you switch, do a temperature tower first, then retraction tower.
Awesome. That’s all for now.
Actually, I lied—one more issue. After all this, my first layer is inconsistent: one side looks squished and the other barely sticks. Is that part of calibration too?

Yes, that’s classic first-layer/bed-mesh calibration. Stringing tuning won’t hold if the first layer is inconsistent because nozzle height varies across the bed. Main causes on an Ender 3 V2: - Bed not trammed (corners not level relative to the nozzle). - X gantry not level (one side higher). - Bed surface warped. - Z offset not set correctly (especially if you changed nozzle/extruder and re-leveled). - Loose eccentric nuts/wheels causing the bed or gantry to rock. Quick path to fix (do in this order): 1) Mechanical sanity: - With steppers disabled, gently try to rock the bed front-left and front-right. If it clunks, tighten the bed carriage eccentric nuts until it’s snug but still rolls smoothly. - Check X gantry: move it near the top and see if left/right height differs. If it sags, adjust the right-side eccentric nut and make sure both sides move smoothly. 2) Tram the bed (manual leveling): - Heat bed to 60 and nozzle to ~190–200 (thermal expansion matters). - Use a paper feeler. Do the 4 corners, then center, then repeat corners once more. 3) Set Z offset: - After tramming, do a first-layer test (big single-layer square) and adjust Z offset live until the line is slightly squished with no gaps, but not so low that it ridges and drags. Do you have a CR Touch/BLTouch, or is it manual leveling only?
I have a CR Touch installed and I use mesh leveling, but it still does this.

Then it’s usually one of these: - Mesh isn’t being loaded/applied at print start. - You trammed poorly and the mesh is trying to compensate too much. - Wrong probe/nozzle offsets or the probe mount is loose. - Z offset drift (especially after nozzle changes). - Mesh was made cold but you print hot (or vice versa). Check your start G-code in Cura. You want something like: - G28 (home) - Then either: - M420 S1 (enable previously saved mesh) or - G29 (probe a new mesh each print) If you do G28 after M420/G29, you’ll wipe out the leveling state. Can you paste your Cura start G-code (just the relevant lines around G28/G29/M420)?

That sequence is fine in principle: home, probe mesh, then purge line. So the mesh should be active. Next likely culprits: 1) Z offset is slightly wrong after your nozzle change. Even with mesh, if Z offset is off, one side can look “barely sticks” because you’re globally too high, and the other looks “too squished” because mesh correction adds/subtracts. Fix: - Re-do Z offset from scratch: - Heat bed 60, nozzle 195. - Home. - Move to center. - Use the firmware’s Z-offset wizard (or babystep) until paper just drags, then go slightly lower for PLA first layer. - Save to EEPROM. 2) Probe mount / bed surface: - Ensure the CR Touch bracket is rigid; any flex changes readings. - Check the bed surface cleanliness: oils can make “barely sticks” look like height issues. Clean with hot water + dish soap, then IPA wipe. 3) Mesh density / mesh range: - If your bed is warped, a coarse mesh can miss local dips/bumps. What mesh size are you using (e.g., 3x3, 5x5, 7x7)?
I think it’s 4x4 or 5x5, not sure. Mriscoc has a menu for it.

If your bed is noticeably uneven, go 5x5 as a good balance. 7x7 can be better but takes longer and may add noise if the probe mount has any wobble. In Mriscoc: - Set mesh to 5x5. - Do a new mesh with the bed and nozzle heated (same temps you print). - Save mesh (if you plan to use M420 S1 instead of G29 each time). Since you already G29 every print, saving isn’t required, but it’s still fine. Then print a single-layer test pattern and do live Z-offset adjustment during the print. For PLA, you want: - Lines touching with no gaps between them, - Slightly flattened top surface, - Not so low that the nozzle plows and leaves rough ridges. When you look at your inconsistent first layer, is the “barely sticks” area always on the same side of the bed?
Yes, the right side is always worse.

That points to either X gantry tilt or bed tilt that the mesh isn’t fully compensating (or probe readings aren’t consistent on that side). Two targeted checks: 1) X gantry level check: - Home Z. - Move the nozzle to the left edge near the bed, lower Z to just touch paper. - Without changing Z, move X to the right edge. If paper drag changes a lot, your gantry is not parallel to the bed motion plane. Ender 3 single-Z machines can droop slightly on the right. Fix options (no new hardware): - Adjust the right-side eccentric nut on the X gantry so the right side is not loose. - Re-square the gantry (loosen and re-tighten the gantry brackets so it sits level). Hardware option: - Dual Z upgrade reduces this issue a lot, but you can often fix it with careful adjustment. 2) Probe repeatability: - In Mriscoc, there’s often a “Probe Repeatability Test.” Run it near the center and near the right side. If variance is bigger on the right, something is flexing or the bed surface there is inconsistent. Do you feel any looseness if you lift the right side of the X gantry by hand (with steppers off)?
Yeah, the right side has a tiny bit of play if I lift it.

That’ll do it. Tighten the right-side X gantry eccentric nut (the wheel adjustment) until the play is gone, but the gantry still moves smoothly up/down without binding. Steps: - Power off or disable steppers. - Hold the gantry so it doesn’t twist. - Turn the eccentric nut in small increments. - After each adjustment, move Z up/down by hand (turn lead screw) and ensure there’s no tight spot. After that: - Re-tram bed (even with CR Touch, tramming matters). - Re-do Z offset. - Re-run mesh. This should significantly improve the right side.
I tightened it and the play is gone. I re-trammed and re-meshed. First layer is much better. Nice. Anything else I should calibrate now that I’m “doing calibration properly”?

A solid Ender 3 calibration stack, in a practical order, is: 1) Mechanics (belts, wheels, gantry square) — you just did a key part. 2) E-steps (you already did after extruder swap). 3) First layer: tram + Z offset + mesh. 4) Flow/extrusion multiplier (so dimensions and seams behave). 5) Temperature (tower) per filament. 6) Retraction/travel/stringing (you already tuned). 7) Linear Advance K (you set ~0.08; refine per filament if you care). 8) Acceleration/jerk (optional; affects ringing and print time). If you want one that often pays off next: flow calibration. Are you currently running Flow in Cura at 100%?
Yes, 100%. How do I calibrate flow without overcomplicating it?

The simplest reliable method is a single-wall thickness test: 1) In Cura: - Make a cube (say 20x20x20). - Set: - Wall line count: 1 - Top layers: 0 - Bottom layers: 0 (optional; can keep 1 bottom if you want stability) - Infill: 0 - Line width: whatever you normally use (0.40 mm) - Print slowly-ish (outer wall 25–30) 2) Print it. 3) Measure wall thickness with calipers in several spots (ignore the seam area). 4) Compute new flow: - New Flow % = Current Flow % * (Expected line width / Measured thickness) Example: expected 0.40, measured 0.44: - New Flow = 100 * (0.40/0.44) ≈ 91% Then reprint to confirm. Notes: - If your slicer uses “Arachne” variable line widths, it can complicate this. Cura has Arachne in newer versions. If enabled, set line width fixed and avoid features that trigger variable width. - Measure away from corners (corners can be thicker). What Cura version are you on?
Cura 5.4. I think it uses Arachne by default.

Cura 5.x does use Arachne. You can still do flow calibration, just reduce variables: - Use a larger cube (30x30) so you have long straight walls. - Keep “Print Thin Walls” off. - Keep line width at 0.40. - Don’t use “Horizontal Expansion” during the test. - Measure mid-wall, mid-height, away from seam. If you find the wall thickness varies a lot around the cube, that can be: - Pressure advance/linear advance interacting, - Inconsistent cooling, - Or the seam bulge affecting measurement. If measurement is stable, the calculation works fine.
I printed it and measured around 0.42 mm average. Expected 0.40. So flow should be about 95%?

Yes: New Flow ≈ 100 * (0.40 / 0.42) ≈ 95.2% Set Flow to 95% and reprint (or print a smaller confirmation piece) to see if you land closer to 0.40. After you set flow, re-check: - Top surface: should still close nicely (if it gets gaps, you went too low). - Seam zits often reduce a bit when you’re not over-extruding. Since you already tuned retraction at flow 100, you might see a tiny change in stringing (often improves slightly). Usually you don’t need to redo everything, just keep an eye on it.
If I set flow to 95 for Hatchbox PLA, do I need a different flow for other PLAs?

Often, yes. Flow is filament- and sometimes even color-specific. Many people keep: - A material profile per brand/type (e.g., Hatchbox PLA: 95%) - Then minor tweaks per spool if needed. If you switch to a different PLA and see: - Overfilled top layers, elephant skin, pronounced seams -> flow might be high. - Gaps between lines, weak top layers -> flow might be low. You don’t have to measure every spool, but it’s the most objective way.
Speaking of elephant skin, I sometimes get elephant foot on the bottom. How do I calibrate that out?

Elephant foot is usually first-layer squish + bed heat + plastic staying soft and spreading. Fixes (in order): 1) Z offset slightly higher (tiny change, like +0.02 to +0.05 mm). 2) Lower bed temp a bit after first layer: - Bed 60 for layer 1, then 55 (or even 50) for the rest. 3) Cura “Initial Layer Horizontal Expansion” (or “Elephant Foot Compensation” depending on version): - Start with -0.2 mm (negative shrinks first layer outline). 4) Reduce initial layer line width if you’re using something huge like 120–140%: - Try 110–120% instead. 5) Make sure first layer speed isn’t too slow with too-hot nozzle; slow can overcook the plastic. Do you currently use a wider initial layer line width (like 120%)?
Yes, initial layer line width is 120%, initial layer height 0.2, speed 20.

That’s a reasonable setup, but if elephant foot is noticeable: - Keep width 120 for adhesion, but try: - Bed: 60 first layer, 55 after - Initial Layer Horizontal Expansion: -0.15 mm (then adjust) And consider raising initial layer speed a bit to 25 if your adhesion stays good; overly slow can make it mushier. If you change Z offset, do it in tiny increments because it also affects adhesion.
Got it. Another calibration thing: my dimensions are slightly off in X and Y, like holes print undersized. Do I calibrate steps/mm for X/Y?

For typical FDM printers, you usually do NOT calibrate X/Y steps/mm based on printed part dimensions, because dimensional error is more often from extrusion width, shrinkage, and hole behavior—not actual motion scaling. X/Y steps are set by belt pitch and pulley teeth and are usually correct unless you changed hardware. For undersized holes: - Use Cura “Hole Horizontal Expansion” (separate from overall expansion). - Or “Horizontal Expansion” (but that affects everything). - Also slow down outer walls and ensure flow is correct (you just set flow, good). - For holes specifically, “Print Outer Wall First” vs “Inner Wall First” can change hole size slightly. Typical starting point: - Hole Horizontal Expansion: +0.1 mm (try +0.15 if still tight) If you need a 5.0 mm hole for an M5 screw clearance, you often model it bigger anyway (like 5.2–5.4) depending on printer. What kind of holes (small like 3–6 mm, or large like 20+ mm)?
Mostly small, like 3 mm and 5 mm holes for screws.

Those are the hardest because they’re close to nozzle width and are dominated by toolpath geometry. Try: - Hole Horizontal Expansion: +0.15 mm - Outer wall speed: 25 - Ensure “Wall Ordering” is consistent; I often prefer inner wall first for better outer surface, but hole accuracy sometimes improves with outer wall first. If Cura has “Print Outer Wall First,” try toggling and test. Also consider: - “Maximum Resolution” / “Deviation” settings in Cura can affect small arcs (segmentation). Defaults are usually okay, but if your holes look faceted, it can matter. A practical test: print a small hole gauge (a plate with labeled holes) and see what expansion value gives the best fit.
Is there a similar calibration for bridging? My bridges sag a bit.

Yes—bridging is a mix of cooling, speed, and flow. In Cura, you can tune: - Bridge Wall Speed / Bridge Skin Speed: slower often helps, but too slow sags more. Many printers like 20–30 mm/s for bridges. - Bridge Fan Speed: 100% for PLA. - Bridge Flow: slightly reduced can help (like 90–95%) so it doesn’t droop under its own weight. - Bridge Skin Density (if present). Also: - Lower nozzle temp a little for bridges (hard to do dynamically unless using plugins); since you’re already around 193–195, you’re in a good zone. If bridges sag a lot: - Check part cooling duct effectiveness; stock duct is okay but not amazing. - Make sure the fan is actually reaching full RPM (some fans are weak). How long are the bridges you’re talking about (5 mm, 20 mm, more)?
Like 15–20 mm across openings.

That’s doable on an Ender 3 with PLA, but it’s near the limit for perfect flat bridges with stock cooling. Try: - Bridge Wall Speed: 25 mm/s - Bridge Skin Speed: 20–25 - Bridge Flow: 92–95% - Fan: 100% - Keep travel fast (doesn’t matter much for bridges, but overall helps) And design-wise, adding a small chamfer or an arch can eliminate the need for a flat 20 mm bridge. If you want a quick diagnostic: print a bridge test that spans 5/10/15/20/25 mm and tune until 15–20 looks acceptable.
I’ll do that. Another weird thing: after I tightened the gantry wheels, I think I introduced slight Z banding. Could that happen?

Yes. Over-tightening wheels or having the gantry bind can create periodic artifacts that look like Z banding. Common causes after adjustments: - Eccentric nuts too tight: wheels deform slightly or bind, causing uneven Z movement. - Z lead screw not aligned: coupler misalignment, lead screw rubbing the motor mount or top guide. - Z axis not moving smoothly: dry lead screw or brass nut issues. - Frame not square. Check: 1) With steppers off, rotate the Z lead screw by hand through the full height. It should feel consistent, not “tight-loose-tight.” 2) Move the gantry up/down and watch the lead screw: does it wobble a lot? Some wobble is normal, but binding is not. 3) Verify the X gantry wheels: you want “snug” (no play) but not hard to turn with finger. A good test: try to spin each wheel with your finger while holding the gantry steady—some should spin with resistance, not locked solid. If it’s binding, slightly back off the eccentric nut you adjusted.
The Z screw feels a little tight around the middle.

That suggests either lead screw alignment issues or the gantry is slightly skewed and binding at that height. Try this: 1) Loosen the two screws holding the brass Z nut to the gantry just a tiny bit (so it can self-align). Don’t remove them; just loosen enough that it can float a fraction. 2) Run Z up and down a few times (via controls) to let it settle. 3) Re-tighten gently (or leave slightly loose—many people leave it a bit floating to reduce binding). Also check the lead screw: - Clean it and apply a light grease (PTFE/silicone-based). Don’t overdo it; a thin film is enough. If you have a top lead screw “stabilizer” bearing, sometimes removing it helps because it forces misalignment. Do you have a top bracket/bearing on the lead screw?
No top bearing. Just the stock setup.

Good—stock is often better than a misaligned top constraint. Do the Z nut float trick and lubrication, and slightly relax the wheel tension if needed. Then print a tall thin test (like a 150 mm tower) and see if banding improves. Also note: some “banding” is actually extrusion inconsistency from temperature fluctuations. But since this appeared after mechanical changes, I’d focus there first.
Okay, I loosened the Z nut screws a bit and lubed the screw. It feels smoother now. I’ll print a tower later.

Great. If the tower still shows periodic bands: - Check if the spacing matches the lead screw pitch (often ~8 mm per revolution on T8 lead screws). If bands repeat every ~8 mm, it’s Z-related. - If bands are random, it’s more likely extrusion/temp.
While we’re deep in calibration: should I tune acceleration and jerk on this printer? I want decent quality without taking forever.

You can, but do it carefully because acceleration/jerk affects ringing and can also influence seam quality (pressure changes). Mriscoc makes it easier to adjust. A practical balanced target for an Ender 3 V2: - Print acceleration: 500–1000 mm/s² - Travel acceleration: 1000–2000 mm/s² - Jerk (if using classic jerk): around 7–10 mm/s (varies by firmware) - Or if it’s “junction deviation” style, you tune that differently. If your prints show ringing (echoes after corners), lower acceleration or jerk. If prints are very slow and corners look overly blobby despite LA, you can increase acceleration a bit. Do you see ringing currently on sharp corners?
A little, yes—like faint ripples after corners.

Then keep acceleration moderate rather than chasing speed. Try: - Print accel: 700 - Travel accel: 1200 - Jerk: 8 And keep outer wall speed at 25–30. Then print a ringing test (a box with sharp corners) and adjust: - If ringing persists: drop accel (e.g., 500). - If ringing is gone but corners are too rounded/slow: you can go up a bit. Also check belt tension—too loose increases ringing; too tight can wear bearings and also create artifacts. Belts should be tight enough to “twang” but not like a guitar string.
Where do I set acceleration—Cura or firmware?

Either works, but don’t fight yourself by setting both inconsistently. Options: 1) Firmware-based (simpler): - Set accel/jerk in Mriscoc menus and let Cura use defaults (no acceleration control). 2) Slicer-based (more granular): - Enable acceleration control in Cura and set different accel for walls/infill/travel. If you’re just starting, I’d set it in firmware first. Once you’re happy, you can optionally use Cura acceleration for fine control (e.g., low accel for outer walls, higher for infill). If you do use Cura acceleration control, make sure “Enable Acceleration Control” is on and values aren’t absurdly high.
I’ll do firmware for now. Another calibration: should I calibrate temperature using PID autotune?

Yes, PID autotune is a good calibration step, especially after changing the hotend/nozzle or if you notice temperature oscillation. Do two PID tunes: - Hotend PID at your typical printing temp (e.g., 200 for PLA). - Bed PID at your bed temp (e.g., 60). On Marlin: - Hotend: M303 E0 S200 C8 - Bed: M303 E-1 S60 C8 (bed syntax can vary; some use E-1, some use U1) Then save with M500. Mriscoc often has PID autotune in menus, which is safer than remembering the exact command. If your temp graph is already stable (±1°C), it’s not urgent, but it’s a nice baseline.
My hotend temp swings about 2 degrees sometimes.

PID tuning will likely tighten that up. A 2°C swing isn’t catastrophic for PLA, but stabilizing it can improve consistency, especially on fine features and bridging. After PID tune, watch: - First layer consistency can improve slightly. - Stringing usually doesn’t change dramatically, but extrusion can look smoother.
I’ll run PID later. Another question: I see “Input Shaping” mentioned online. Does Mriscoc have that?

Traditional Marlin on 8-bit/most stock Ender 3 V2 setups typically doesn’t have full input shaping like Klipper. Some newer Marlin branches have experimental features, but it’s not commonly available/used the same way. Mriscoc firmware focuses more on usability, menus, leveling, and general tuning. If you want input shaping, most people move to Klipper (requires a host like a Raspberry Pi or similar). Given you’re already getting good quality at moderate accel, input shaping is optional. Linear Advance + reasonable accel gets you far.
Fair. Let’s talk about bed adhesion calibration. Sometimes corners lift slightly on bigger prints.

Corner lift/warping is mostly thermal and adhesion. For PLA on an Ender bed: 1) Cleanliness: - Wash build surface with dish soap + warm water, rinse, dry. - Then IPA wipe before prints. 2) First layer: - Ensure Z offset is correct. - Initial layer speed 20–25 is fine. 3) Bed temp: - 60 first layer, 55 after can reduce elephant foot while still sticking. If corners lift, keep 60 longer (maybe first 5 layers) or keep 60 for the whole print. 4) Drafts: - Avoid cold air, fans, or AC hitting the printer. 5) Brim: - Use a 5–8 mm brim for large flat parts. 6) Cooling: - Full fan too early can cause warp on some geometries. You can ramp fan: 0% layer 1–2, 50% layer 3, 100% by layer 5. Do your lifted corners happen on thin flat parts (like trays) or general models?
Mostly thin flat parts, like a phone stand base.

Those are warp-prone. Use: - Brim: 8–10 lines (or 6–8 mm) - Bed: 60 throughout (at least for those parts) - Fan ramp: 0% layer 1, 30% layer 2, 60% layer 3, 100% layer 5 And consider adding “mouse ears” (small discs) at corners if the model allows. Also, if you’re on glass: - A thin layer of glue stick can help as both adhesion and release. On textured PEI, usually cleaning is enough. What surface are you printing on (stock glass/carborundum, PEI, etc.)?
Stock glass with the textured side.

That surface can work well, but it’s sensitive to oils and sometimes needs a “reset” cleaning. Best practice: - Take it off, wash with dish soap and a non-scratch sponge, rinse thoroughly. - Avoid touching the print area with fingers afterward. - IPA wipe before a big print. If corners still lift on large thin parts, glue stick is a reasonable tool—not because the bed can’t stick, but because it makes adhesion more uniform across the plate.
Okay. Back to mesh leveling: should I probe every print with G29, or just load a saved mesh?

Either is fine; tradeoffs: - G29 every print: - Pros: compensates for small changes (temperature, bed movement, removing/placing glass). - Cons: adds time; probe wear; if something is slightly loose, you may get inconsistent meshes. - Saved mesh (M420 S1): - Pros: faster starts; consistent if your bed setup is stable. - Cons: if anything changes (bed knobs moved, glass removed), mesh may be wrong. A practical approach: - If you remove the glass or adjust knobs often: G29. - If your setup is stable: create a good heated mesh, save it, and use M420 S1. You can also do: G28, M420 S1, and only re-mesh weekly or after you touch the bed.
I do remove the glass to clean it sometimes.

Then either re-mesh after reinstalling (recommended) or keep G29 in start code. If you keep G29, just make sure: - The nozzle is clean before probing (a blob on the nozzle can change trigger height). - Bed and nozzle are heated consistently. Some people add a small “nozzle wipe” routine before G29, but that’s optional if you keep the nozzle clean manually.
My nozzle sometimes has a little ooze right before probing. Is that a problem?

It can be. If filament is hanging from the nozzle, the nozzle can touch the bed before the probe triggers (or drag), skewing Z offset and mesh. Solutions: - Heat nozzle, then do a short retract before probing (like 2–3 mm) to reduce ooze. - Add a wipe line or “wipe on brush” routine before probing (if you have a brush). - Set probing temp slightly lower than printing temp (e.g., probe at 170–180, then heat to 195 for printing). That reduces ooze a lot. Simplest: probe at 170–180°C for PLA, bed at 60. After G29, heat to print temp and purge line. If you change probing temp, re-check Z offset because thermal expansion changes slightly.
That’s interesting. How would I change that in start G-code?

One straightforward pattern: - Set bed temp and start heating nozzle to a lower “probe temp” - Home + mesh - Then heat nozzle to print temp - Purge line Example (adapt to your temps): M140 S60        ; set bed temp (no wait) M104 S175       ; set nozzle to probe temp (no wait) M190 S60        ; wait for bed M109 S175       ; wait for nozzle at probe temp G28 G29 M104 S193       ; set nozzle to print temp M109 S193       ; wait for print temp ; purge line... (then your G92/G1 moves) This reduces ooze during probing. If you do this, confirm your first layer again and adjust Z offset if needed.
Great. Another calibration: I hear about “extrusion temperature vs speed.” Should I tune that?

It’s useful if you print at widely different speeds. The faster you print, the more heat is needed to melt filament fast enough (volumetric flow). If you mostly print around 50 mm/s, you’re fine with a single temp. If you sometimes print fast infill (80–100) or bigger nozzles/layer heights, you might need: - Slightly higher temp for those high-flow profiles - Or cap volumetric flow (Cura has a “Maximum Volumetric Speed” setting) For stock Ender 3 hotend with 0.4 nozzle, PLA practical volumetric flow is often around 8–10 mm³/s before quality drops (varies). If you want to sanity-check: - At 0.2 layer height, 0.4 line width, 50 mm/s: flow = 0.2*0.4*50 = 4 mm³/s (easy) - At 0.28 layer height, 0.48 width, 80 mm/s: flow = 10.75 mm³/s (near limit) So you only need to tune temp vs speed if you push those higher-flow regimes.
That calculation helps. I mostly do 0.2 and 50, so I’m safe.
Then your current temp tuning (around 193–195) is in a comfortable range and should stay consistent.
I printed the Z banding tower; it looks better now. Slight bands remain but much less.

That’s a good outcome. The remaining faint bands can be normal for this class of printer. If you want to chase the last bit: - Make sure filament spool feeds smoothly (no snags). - Check extruder gear cleanliness and idler tension. - Ensure hotend fan is healthy (heat creep can cause intermittent flow changes). - Consider printing outer walls a bit slower (25) and keep acceleration moderate. If the band spacing is irregular, it’s more likely extrusion variability than Z mechanics.
The spacing is irregular, so probably extrusion.

Then focus on: - Filament path friction: spool holder, sharp bends in Bowden. - Extruder gear: clean teeth, correct tension (not crushing the filament). - Temperature stability: do PID tune. - Filament consistency: some spools vary. A cheap improvement: mount the spool so it unwinds smoothly with minimal tug. Even small tension changes can show up as banding on tall prints.
I think my spool holder is sticky. It’s the stock top-mounted one.

Top-mounted stock holder can add drag and also wobble the frame slightly as the spool jerks. Two easy options: - Add a roller-style spool holder with bearings (many printable designs). - Move spool off the top to a side mount or separate stand. Even without printing a new holder, you can: - Make sure the spool rotates freely (a little PTFE washer helps). - Ensure filament comes off the spool without rubbing the frame.
I’ll print a bearing spool holder now that my printer is behaving. Anything I should watch out for when printing functional parts like that?

For functional parts (spool holder arms, bearing mounts): - Use 3–4 walls and 30–40% infill (Gyroid or Grid). - Print orientation matters: align layer lines so the part isn’t loaded in layer-separation direction. - PLA is okay, but PETG is better for heat resistance if it’s near the printer’s warm areas. For a spool holder, PLA is usually fine. Also ensure holes for bearings are sized appropriately; you may need that hole expansion tweak.
Good point. I’ll do 4 walls, 40% gyroid. By the way, does gyroid infill affect calibration or quality?

It can affect: - Print smoothness (gyroid is continuous, fewer sharp starts/stops; often quieter). - Strength more isotropically. - Less nozzle catching vs lines in some cases. It doesn’t change core calibration values, but it can reduce the “nozzle hits infill” issue you saw.
After all these changes, should I save a “baseline profile” in Cura?

Yes, and it’s worth doing two profiles: 1) PLA quality baseline (what you tuned) 2) PLA functional/faster baseline (slightly higher speeds/infill, same retraction/temp) For the baseline profile, include: - Temps (193–195, bed 60/55) - Retraction (6.0 @ 45, prime 38, min travel 0.8–1.0) - Travel (200) - Combing (Within infill or Not in skin) - Seam alignment (Back/Sharpest) - Flow (95% for Hatchbox PLA) - Fan ramp - Start G-code with probe temp if you adopt it Then export the profile so you can restore it after Cura updates.
I set flow to 95 and now top layers have tiny gaps sometimes. Did I go too low?

Possibly. Single-wall flow calibration can overshoot if measurement included seam bulge or if Arachne altered line widths. Two ways to correct without undoing everything: Option A (simple): - Raise flow slightly: try 97% and re-check top surface. Often the “true” value lands between the wall test result and 100. Option B (separate top/bottom flow): - Cura has “Top/Bottom Flow” in some setups. Keep overall flow at 95–97 but set top/bottom to 100–102 to ensure closure. This is useful if you’re dialing flow down mainly to fix seams but still want solid tops. Before changing, confirm: - How many top layers and what top thickness? For 0.2 layer height, I like at least 0.8 mm top thickness (4 layers). If you’re at 3 layers (0.6), gaps are more likely. What are your top layers set to?
Top layers is 3.

Increase to 4 top layers (or top thickness 0.8 mm). That alone often fixes minor pinholes. Then set flow to 97% and test. If seams get worse, you can keep 95–96 and set Top/Bottom Flow to 100. Also ensure: - “Ironing” is off while you’re diagnosing; it can mask or worsen top surface issues depending.
I’ll do 4 top layers and flow 97. Thanks. This is a lot of calibration, but it’s finally making sense.

Once you have these baselines locked, day-to-day printing becomes much simpler: you mostly adjust temperature per filament and maybe minor retraction tweaks, while the mechanical/first-layer foundation stays stable.
If I wanted to document this for myself, what are the must-record parameters?

Record these per filament/profile: Machine/firmware: - Firmware version, Linear Advance K, accel/jerk values, E-steps Filament: - Brand/type/color, drying notes Slicer core: - Nozzle temp, bed temp (first layer and rest) - Layer height, line width - Flow % - Speeds: outer wall, inner wall, infill, travel - Retraction: distance, retract speed, prime speed, min travel, combing mode, wipe on/off, Z-hop - Cooling: fan ramp details - Bed leveling method: G29 each print or M420 S1, probe temp approach And note any special fixes: - Hole horizontal expansion value you like - Elephant foot compensation used That’s enough to recreate results quickly.
Perfect. I think I’m done for real now.
If something drifts later, the first checks are always: extruder tension, nozzle condition, bed cleanliness, and Z offset/mesh. Those four solve a surprising percentage of “mystery” print issues.
I have a BME280 sensor and an Arduino Uno. How do I wire it up and read temperature data?

If your BME280 breakout supports I2C (most do), that’s the easiest path on an Arduino Uno. Wiring (I2C): - BME280 VIN/VCC -> Arduino 3.3V (preferred). Many breakouts tolerate 5V because they include a regulator/level shifting, but check your board. If it has a pin labeled “3Vo” or “3V3” and “VIN”, use VIN to 5V; otherwise use 3.3V. - BME280 GND -> Arduino GND - BME280 SCL -> Arduino A5 (SCL) - BME280 SDA -> Arduino A4 (SDA) Optional: - BME280 CS -> leave unconnected for I2C (or tie high depending on board; usually not needed) - BME280 SDO -> sets I2C address. GND = 0x76, VCC = 0x77 (common) Software: 1) Install a library: - “Adafruit BME280 Library” (and “Adafruit Unified Sensor”) via Library Manager. 2) Minimal example (Adafruit library):
I’m not sure if my breakout is 3.3V or 5V. It has pins labeled VIN, GND, SCK, SDI, SDO, CS. No SDA/SCL labels. Does that change anything?

That pin labeling suggests the board is exposing SPI names rather than I2C names, but many BME280 breakouts support both SPI and I2C. The key is that: - SCK is the same physical line as SCL in I2C mode. - SDI is the same as SDA (data in to the sensor; in I2C it’s bidirectional). - SDO is used in SPI as MISO; in I2C it typically selects the address (0x76/0x77). - CS selects SPI; for I2C you usually tie CS high (to VIN/VCC) so the chip ignores SPI and uses I2C. So you can usually do I2C even if the pins are labeled SPI. Wiring for I2C with your pins: - VIN -> Arduino 3.3V or 5V depending on breakout (we’ll resolve in a second) - GND -> GND - SCK -> A5 (SCL) - SDI -> A4 (SDA) - CS -> VIN (pull high) or leave unconnected if the breakout already pulls it up; but safest: tie CS to VIN. - SDO -> GND for address 0x76 (or to VIN for 0x77) How to tell power: - If the board has a regulator and level shifters, it often says “VIN” (not “VCC”) and accepts 3–5V. If it’s a bare module, it might say “VCC” and be 3.3V only. - Look for components: a small 3-pin regulator (often marked “662K” or similar) and a couple of MOSFETs for level shifting. If present, 5V is usually OK. - If unsure, power from Arduino 3.3V to be safe. BME280 itself is a 3.3V part, so 3.3V avoids risking damage. Next step: run an I2C scanner to confirm it’s seen and what address it uses.
Okay, I’ll do the I2C scanner. Can you give me the code and what I should see?
Sure. Upload this to the Uno, open Serial Monitor at 9600 baud, and it will scan all 7-bit I2C addresses. Code (I2C scanner):
I uploaded the scanner and it prints “No I2C devices found”. I wired SCK to A5 and SDI to A4, VIN to 3.3V, GND to GND, CS to VIN, SDO to GND. Any ideas?

A few common gotchas to check systematically: 1) Confirm you’re using the right Arduino pins - Uno: SDA is A4, SCL is A5. That part is right. - Make sure you didn’t accidentally swap A4/A5. 2) Check power and ground continuity - Measure with a multimeter if you have one: VIN pin on the breakout should read ~3.3V relative to GND. - Ensure GND is common. A single loose jumper can cause “no devices found”. 3) Pull-ups on SDA/SCL - I2C needs pull-up resistors on SDA and SCL. Many breakouts include them. If yours doesn’t, the lines may float. - If you have 4.7k resistors, add: SDA to 3.3V via 4.7k, SCL to 3.3V via 4.7k. - However, most BME280 breakouts do include pull-ups, so this might not be it. 4) CS behavior - For I2C mode, CS must be high. You tied it to VIN, good. - Double-check you tied CS to VIN, not to 3.3V rail that isn’t actually VIN if you used 3.3V—though that should still be “high”. 5) SDO address select - If SDO isn’t firmly tied, address could be something unexpected? Usually only 0x76 or 0x77. - You tied to GND, so should be 0x76. Try tying SDO to VIN and scan again (would become 0x77). 6) Board might be SPI-only (rare, but possible) - Some breakouts don’t bring out I2C properly, but with BME280 it’s usually multi-protocol. 7) Sensor orientation / pin order confusion - Some boards label SDI/SDO opposite of what you expect, but for I2C SDI should be SDA. Quick test checklist: - Swap SCK/SDI lines (A4/A5) just to see if anything shows up. - Try powering VIN from 5V instead of 3.3V if your breakout has a regulator (VIN suggests it might). If it’s a regulator board, 5V is fine and sometimes the 3.3V pin on the Uno can’t supply enough current for some modules (usually BME280 is low current, but still worth trying). - Try a different I2C device (if you have one) to confirm the Uno I2C pins are working. If you can describe the breakout (any markings, presence of a regulator chip), I can guess whether 5V VIN is expected.
The breakout has a tiny chip labeled “AMS1117-3.3” and a couple of small transistors. So I guess it has a regulator and level shifting. Should I use 5V then?

Yes—if it has an AMS1117-3.3 regulator and level-shifting components, it’s designed to accept 5V on VIN and provide 3.3V to the sensor internally. In that case, wire: - VIN -> 5V - GND -> GND - SCK -> A5 - SDI -> A4 - CS -> VIN (still fine) - SDO -> GND (0x76) or VIN (0x77) Then rerun the scanner. Also, with those boards the I2C pull-ups might be to 3.3V internally, which is correct. If it still finds nothing, I’d suspect either: - SDA/SCL not actually routed as SDI/SCK on that specific board (unusual), or - a wiring/breadboard issue. After switching to 5V, tell me what the scanner prints.
Now it finds a device at 0x76. Great. How do I read temperature and humidity?

Great—now you can use the BME280 library. Using Adafruit_BME280 (simple and common): 1) Install: - Adafruit BME280 Library - Adafruit Unified Sensor 2) Wire stays the same (I2C). Example code: - Include libraries - Initialize with address 0x76 - Print temp (°C), humidity (%), pressure (hPa) You can then convert °C to °F if desired. If you want, I can paste a full sketch with periodic readings and a nicer serial output format.
Yes please paste a full sketch. Also I want it to output Fahrenheit too.

Here’s a complete Uno sketch using I2C at address 0x76, printing every 2 seconds: (Full sketch) - Initializes Serial at 115200 - bme.begin(0x76) - Reads: temperatureC, temperatureF, humidity, pressurePa - Converts pressure to hPa and inches Hg optionally If you want inches of mercury, I can add that too.
Add inches of mercury and also altitude estimate. I know altitude needs sea level pressure; can I just use 1013.25?

You can, with the caveat that altitude will be approximate because sea-level pressure changes with weather. But for a beginner weather station it’s fine to start with 1013.25 hPa. I’ll add: - pressure_hPa = pressurePa / 100.0 - pressure_inHg = pressure_hPa * 0.0295299830714 - altitude_m = bme.readAltitude(SEALEVELPRESSURE_HPA) Full sketch with constants: - #define SEALEVELPRESSURE_HPA (1013.25) Would you like altitude in feet too?
Yes altitude in feet. Also the readings jump around a bit. Can we smooth them?

Sure. Two common approaches: 1) Simple moving average over the last N samples (easy and stable). 2) Exponential moving average (EMA) (less memory, responsive). For a weather station, EMA is a good fit: - filtered = alpha * new + (1 - alpha) * filtered Where alpha maybe 0.1–0.3 depending on smoothing desired. We can maintain filtered values for temp, humidity, pressure, altitude. The altitude is derived from pressure; better to filter pressure then compute altitude from filtered pressure for consistency. I’ll modify code: - Keep filteredPressure_hPa (EMA) - Use filtered pressure for inHg and altitude - Keep filtered temp/humidity too Pick alpha: try 0.2. If too sluggish, increase to 0.3; if too jittery, decrease to 0.1.
That makes sense. Can you show the code with EMA? Also I’m curious: should I oversample in the sensor settings?

Yes, the BME280 has configurable oversampling and an IIR filter inside the sensor. That’s often better than doing everything in Arduino code, or you can combine both. Typical “weather monitoring” recommended settings: - Temperature oversampling: x2 - Pressure oversampling: x16 - Humidity oversampling: x1 - IIR filter: 16 - Standby time: 500 ms or 1000 ms Adafruit library supports setting sampling via: bme.setSampling(mode, tempSampling, pressSampling, humSampling, filter, standby); Then you can read at ~1 Hz or slower. I’ll provide a sketch that: - Sets sampling to weather mode-ish - Reads every 2 seconds - Applies EMA smoothing on top
Cool. Now I want to add a rain sensor and an anemometer later, but for now I want to log the BME280 data to an SD card. I have an SD card module. How do I wire that to the Uno?

SD modules typically use SPI. The Uno SPI pins are: - MOSI: D11 - MISO: D12 - SCK: D13 - CS: commonly D10 (but can be any digital pin) Wiring (SD module -> Uno): - VCC -> 5V (if module has regulator/level shifting). If it’s a bare 3.3V module, use 3.3V and level shifting. - GND -> GND - SCK -> D13 - MISO -> D12 - MOSI -> D11 - CS -> D10 (or your chosen pin) Important: - Many cheap SD modules are 5V-friendly (regulator + level shifting). Some are not. If it says “3.3V only”, don’t feed 5V logic. - Use short wires; SPI can be sensitive. Software: - Use the built-in SD library. - Initialize: SD.begin(chipSelectPin) - Open a file and append lines like CSV. Do you know if your SD module says “Catalex” or has a 74HC4050 level shifter? If not sure, describe the board markings/pins.
It says “Micro SD Card Adapter” and has pins: CS, SCK, MOSI, MISO, VCC, GND. There’s a chip labeled “AMS1117-3.3” again.

That’s a good sign: AMS1117-3.3 suggests it takes 5V on VCC and regulates to 3.3V for the card. Many such modules also include level shifting (sometimes resistor dividers, sometimes a buffer). It’s usually intended for 5V Arduino use. Wire it: - VCC -> 5V - GND -> GND - CS -> D10 - SCK -> D13 - MOSI -> D11 - MISO -> D12 Then test SD alone with the SD library “CardInfo” example first. After that, integrate logging. One more detail: because you’re also using I2C for the BME280, it’s fine—SPI and I2C can coexist. Just don’t reuse the same pins.
I tested CardInfo and it works. How do I design the data format? I want a CSV with timestamp, tempF, humidity, pressure, and maybe raw values too.

On an Uno you don’t have a real-time clock by default, so “timestamp” options: 1) Use millis() since start (easy, relative time). 2) Add an RTC module (DS3231) for real date/time (better). 3) If you later add a serial connection to a computer, you can sync time at startup. For now, a practical CSV: - ms_since_start - temp_C_raw, temp_F_raw - humidity_raw - pressure_hPa_raw, pressure_inHg_raw - temp_C_filt, temp_F_filt - humidity_filt - pressure_hPa_filt, pressure_inHg_filt But that’s a lot of columns. You could keep it simpler: - ms - tempF - humidity - pressure_hPa And optionally add filtered values only. Implementation tips: - Write a header line once if file is new. - Flush occasionally, but not every line to reduce wear and time (SD writes are slow). - Buffer a line in a char array and write it. If you want, I can provide an integrated sketch that: - Reads sensor - Applies EMA - Logs to “weather.csv” - Creates file and header if missing - Prints to Serial too
Please do the integrated sketch. Also I heard SD can cause problems with memory. How do we keep it stable?

Key stability tips on Uno (2KB RAM): - Avoid String objects; use fixed char buffers and dtostrf for floats. - Keep libraries minimal. - Don’t keep large arrays. - Write line-by-line, don’t store lots of data in RAM. - Use F() macro for constant strings in Serial prints. In the sketch I’ll: - Use a char line[120] buffer - Use snprintf where possible (though AVR snprintf float support is limited). So for floats, I’ll either: - use dtostrf to convert floats to strings, then assemble, or - print directly to the file using file.print(value, digits) to avoid formatting to buffer. Direct printing is simplest and memory-light. We’ll do: file.print(millis()); file.print(','); file.print(tempF, 2); ... That avoids heavy formatting. We’ll also: - Open file, write, close each interval (safe but slower), or keep it open and flush periodically (faster but risk corruption on sudden power loss). Beginner-friendly: open/write/close each time. If logging every 2 seconds, it’s okay. I’ll provide that version.
I tried combining the code and it compiles but sometimes it hangs after a few minutes. Serial stops printing. Any debugging steps?

Common causes when SD + sensors run together: 1) Power issues - SD cards can draw bursts of current (tens to 100+ mA). If powered from the Uno’s 5V via USB, it might be marginal depending on cable/port. - Try a powered USB hub or a stable 5V supply. - Add a decoupling capacitor near SD module: 100 µF electrolytic + 0.1 µF ceramic across VCC/GND. 2) SPI chip select handling - Ensure SD CS pin is set to OUTPUT and HIGH when not used. - If any other SPI device is present (not in your case yet), all other CS must be HIGH during SD access. - Even with only SD, set pinMode(10, OUTPUT) because on Uno, if SS pin (D10) becomes INPUT, SPI can switch to slave mode. 3) File open/close patterns - Opening/closing too frequently can be okay, but if you’re doing it very fast or failing to close on error, you can lock up. - Add error checks and print error codes. 4) I2C bus lockups (less common with BME280) - If I2C lines get disturbed, Wire library can hang. Keeping wires short helps. 5) Memory fragmentation - Using String can fragment heap and crash over time. Avoid String. Debug steps: - Add a “heartbeat” LED toggle each loop so you can see if loop is running. - Print free RAM periodically (there are small helper functions). - Wrap SD operations with timing and error prints. - If it hangs, see whether it hangs at SD open, SD write, or sensor read by adding prints before/after each action. Great. Now I want to add an LCD. I have a 16x2 LCD with an I2C backpack. Can I use it on the same I2C bus as the BME280?

Yes, I2C is a shared bus, so multiple devices can coexist as long as they have different addresses. Typical addresses: - BME280: 0x76 or 0x77 - LCD backpack (PCF8574): often 0x27 or 0x3F Wiring: - LCD VCC -> 5V (most backpacks are 5V) - LCD GND -> GND - LCD SDA -> A4 - LCD SCL -> A5 Because you already have BME280 on A4/A5, just connect LCD SDA/SCL in parallel. Then run I2C scanner again; you should see two addresses. Software: - Use LiquidCrystal_I2C library (there are variants; sometimes you need the right one). - Initialize with address (0x27) and 16,2. - Update display every second or so (not too fast to reduce flicker). One caution: your BME280 breakout likely has level shifting and is fine at 5V I2C; LCD backpack is 5V. This should work together. If you had a bare 3.3V sensor without level shifting, you’d need to ensure pull-ups go to 3.3V, not 5V.
I scanned and found 0x76 and 0x27. Nice. How should I layout the LCD? I want line 1: tempF and humidity, line 2: pressure inHg and maybe a trend arrow.

That’s a good layout for 16x2. Example formatting: Line 1: "T:72.4F H:45%" Line 2: "P:29.92in  ↑" Trend arrow: - Keep last filtered pressure value and compare to current filtered pressure. - If change > threshold (like 0.02 inHg over last N minutes or last few samples), show up/down; else dash. Because you’re sampling every 2 seconds, comparing adjacent samples will be noisy. Better: - Keep a slow trend reference: store pressure from, say, 5 minutes ago (or 60 samples if 5 sec interval). On Uno, storing 60 floats is too much RAM. Instead: - Use another EMA with very small alpha as “long-term” baseline and compare short-term vs long-term. Or: - Store a single “pressure_5min_ago” updated every 5 minutes. Simple approach: - Every 60 seconds, update a reference pressureRef = currentFilteredPressure. - Trend = currentFilteredPressure - pressureRef. Then arrow based on trend threshold. Before you paste everything, can you explain how to avoid delays? I see many examples with delay(2000). Why is millis better here?

delay() blocks the CPU—nothing else runs while waiting. In your project you have multiple periodic tasks: - Read sensor - Update LCD - Write SD - Possibly handle buttons later If you use delay(2000), you can’t update the LCD every 1 second while also logging every 2 seconds cleanly, and you can’t respond to button presses promptly. Using millis(): - You store timestamps of when you last did each task. - In loop(), you check if enough time has passed for each task. - If yes, do the task and update its last timestamp. - The loop keeps spinning quickly, allowing multiple schedules to coexist. This pattern scales well as you add more sensors and UI.

Great. One more thing: I see pressure drifting if I touch the sensor board. Should I mount it differently?

Touching the board warms it slightly and can affect temperature, which can slightly affect pressure compensation. Also your finger can apply pressure to the sensor port if you cover it. Mounting tips: - Keep the sensor exposed to air, not enclosed tightly. - Avoid placing it near heat sources (voltage regulators, Uno’s USB chip, SD module). - Use standoffs and mount the BME280 away from the Uno by a few centimeters on wires. - If inside an enclosure, add ventilation holes near the sensor. - Don’t cover the metal can opening of the BME280. - Use foam or rubber grommets if you want to reduce vibration, but keep airflow. If you later add a solar radiation shield or a small aspirated enclosure, temperature readings will be better.
Makes sense. Now I want to plan ahead: adding an anemometer and wind vane. I found a cheap set with reed switch for speed and resistive ladder for direction. How would that connect to the Uno?

Typical weather station anemometer: - Speed: reed switch closes once per rotation (or twice) producing pulses. Connect to an interrupt-capable pin (D2 or D3 on Uno). - Use INPUT_PULLUP and count pulses with an interrupt service routine (ISR). - Compute RPM and then wind speed using the anemometer’s calibration factor. Wind vane: - Usually a set of resistors switched by reed contacts, producing different voltages. Connect to an analog input (A0). - Use a voltage divider with a pull-up (often 10k to 5V) and read analog value, map ranges to directions. Wiring speed sensor: - One wire to D2 - Other to GND - pinMode(D2, INPUT_PULLUP) - attachInterrupt(digitalPinToInterrupt(2), isr, FALLING) Debounce: - Reed switch bounces; you can ignore pulses that occur too quickly (e.g., within 5–10 ms) in ISR using micros(). Wiring direction sensor: - Vane common to GND, output to A0 with pull-up to 5V (or vice versa depending on model). - Then read analogRead(A0) and map to 16 directions. You’ll want to check the datasheet for your specific model: number of pulses per revolution and voltage table for directions.

A better trend indicator is based on a longer window (like 10–30 minutes) because pressure changes slowly. Options without using lots of RAM: 1) Dual-EMA method: - fastPressure = EMA(alphaFast ~ 0.2) - slowPressure = EMA(alphaSlow ~ 0.01) (equivalent to a long time constant) - trend = fastPressure - slowPressure This gives a stable “rising/falling” indication. 2) Periodic snapshot: - Store pressureRef updated every 10 minutes. - trend = current - pressureRef This is stable but updates stepwise. I recommend dual-EMA. Then choose thresholds: - If trend > +0.02 inHg => rising - If trend < -0.02 inHg => falling - else steady You can tune thresholds. That’s good. I think my project is getting big. Should I move to an Arduino Mega or ESP32?

Depends on your goals: Arduino Uno: - Pros: simple, lots of examples, stable. - Cons: limited RAM/flash; SD + LCD + multiple sensors can get tight. Arduino Mega 2560: - Pros: much more RAM (8KB) and flash, more pins, easy migration. - Cons: still 8-bit, no built-in Wi-Fi. ESP32: - Pros: lots of RAM, fast, Wi-Fi/Bluetooth, can do NTP time, web server, OTA updates. - Cons: 3.3V logic (need level shifting for some 5V modules), library differences, more complex. If you plan to: - Add RTC, anemometer, wind vane, rain gauge, maybe display menus, plus data logging and maybe wireless upload then Mega is a straightforward next step. If you want cloud dashboards, web UI, NTP timestamps, ESP32 is ideal. You can also keep the Uno for now and design your wiring in a way that ports easily (use I2C/SPI/interrupt pins that exist on both).
I want Wi-Fi later for uploading data. But I also want to keep it beginner-friendly. Is ESP32 too hard?

It’s manageable if you take it in steps: 1) First get BME280 working on ESP32 over I2C. 2) Then add SD (ESP32 supports SPI; pick pins). 3) Then add Wi-Fi and NTP time. 4) Then upload via HTTP/MQTT. Main beginner differences: - ESP32 is 3.3V. Your BME280 and many SD modules are fine. Your LCD backpack may be 5V; some work at 3.3V I2C, but not always. Better to use a 3.3V-compatible display or ensure proper pull-ups. - Pin mapping differs; you choose I2C pins (default often GPIO21 SDA, GPIO22 SCL). - The Arduino IDE support is good, but you’ll add the ESP32 board package. If you want, I can outline a migration plan and list which of your current modules will work directly at 3.3V.
Let’s keep Uno for now. I have one more issue: sometimes the SD file gets corrupted if I unplug power. How can I reduce that?

SD corruption on sudden power loss is common. Mitigations: - Close the file after each write (you’re doing that). That helps a lot because it finalizes directory entries. - Avoid keeping the file open for long periods. - Ensure stable power; brownouts during write are a big cause. Add capacitance near SD module (100–470 µF). - Consider writing to a new file each day/session rather than one huge file (reduces directory update complexity and fragmentation). - Use a “safe shutdown” button or detect low voltage (more advanced). - Reduce write frequency (every 5–10 seconds) if acceptable. Even with close-after-write, a sudden power loss during the write can still corrupt the last line or, occasionally, filesystem metadata. Using a quality SD card helps. If you want maximum robustness: - Write to a temporary file and periodically rename (more complex). - Or write fixed-size records (binary log) and post-process (more complex). For now: add a capacitor and maybe increase log interval to 5 seconds.
I’ll add a capacitor and keep 2 seconds for now. Can you help me add a simple checksum to each CSV line so I can detect partial writes?

Yes. A lightweight approach: - Compute an 8-bit XOR checksum over the characters in the CSV payload (excluding newline). - Append it as an extra field, e.g. “,CS=5A”. Then in post-processing you can verify. Implementation on Arduino: - As you print each field, you’d need to also feed the same characters into a checksum function. That’s tricky if you directly file.print floats. Alternative: - Build the line into a char buffer (without String) using dtostrf + strcat, then compute checksum on the buffer, then write buffer + checksum. But float formatting on AVR can be finicky. Compromise: - Use integer scaled values to make formatting simple: - tempF_x100 = (int)(tempF*100) - hum_x100 - press_inHg_x1000 Then line is integers, easy with snprintf (no float). Example CSV: ms,tempF_x100,hum_x100,pressInHg_x1000,cs This is robust and smaller. Would you accept scaled integers instead of human-friendly decimals?
Yes, scaled integers are fine. Let’s do tempF x100, humidity x100, pressure inHg x1000. Include filtered values only.

Great. Then we can use snprintf reliably (integers only), compute XOR checksum, and write one line. Line format: ms,tempF_x100,hum_x100,pressInHg_x1000,cs_hex Example: 123456,7245,4532,29920,5A Checksum: - XOR of all bytes in the line up to the last comma before checksum (or XOR of everything before “,cs”). We’ll define it clearly: Compute XOR over the characters of “ms,tempF_x100,hum_x100,pressInHg_x1000” (no trailing comma), then append “,CS” field as hex. In practice: - Build payload string without checksum - checksum = xorBytes(payload) - line = payload + "," + hex(checksum) + "\n" I’ll show code for: - build payload with snprintf - compute checksum - append checksum

Great. One last question: my BME280 temperature seems a couple degrees higher than expected. Is that normal?

It can be, especially if the sensor is near warm components or not well ventilated. Common reasons: - Self-heating from the breakout’s regulator or from being close to the Uno. - Heat from the SD module during writes. - Enclosure trapping heat. - Measurement differences vs another thermometer (calibration, placement, response time). Fixes: - Physically separate the BME280 from heat sources using longer wires. - Ensure airflow around the sensor. - Reduce sampling rate/oversampling a bit to reduce sensor self-heating (usually minor). - Apply a calibration offset in software (e.g., subtract 1.5°F) after you’ve improved mounting and compared against a reliable reference. If you want, we can add a configurable offset constant in code for temperature and humidity calibration.

Typically: tempF_corrected = tempF_raw + TEMP_F_OFFSET; hum_corrected = hum_raw + HUM_OFFSET; Then filter corrected values.
Thanks. Can you summarize the final system architecture and pin connections so I can write it down?

Pins and buses: I2C bus (shared): - Uno A4 (SDA) -> BME280 SDI (SDA) and LCD SDA - Uno A5 (SCL) -> BME280 SCK (SCL) and LCD SCL Power: - BME280 VIN -> 5V (regulator board) - BME280 GND -> GND - BME280 CS -> VIN (forces I2C mode) - BME280 SDO -> GND (address 0x76) - LCD VCC -> 5V - LCD GND -> GND SPI bus (SD module): - Uno D13 (SCK) -> SD SCK - Uno D11 (MOSI) -> SD MOSI - Uno D12 (MISO) -> SD MISO - Uno D10 (CS) -> SD CS Power: - SD VCC -> 5V (regulator board) - SD GND -> GND Add capacitor near SD: 100–470 µF across VCC/GND. Button: - Uno D2 -> one side of button - Other side -> GND - Use INPUT_PULLUP in code. Status LED: - LED_BUILTIN (D13) used as heartbeat; note D13 is also SCK. Toggling LED while using SPI can be okay but sometimes causes noise depending on board; if you see SPI issues, move heartbeat to another pin + external LED/resistor (e.g., D4). Software tasks (non-blocking using millis): - Sensor read every 1s (apply offsets, update fast/slow EMA, update min/max) - LCD update every 1s (screen based on mode) - SD log every 2s (write scaled integers + checksum, open/write/close) - Button handler runs continuously (debounce, short/long press) If you want, I can adjust heartbeat LED to avoid D13 conflicts and use D4 instead.

I pasted it but got a compile error: “no matching function for call to ‘Adafruit_BME280::setSampling(...)’”. What’s the correct call signature?

That means your Adafruit_BME280 library version expects a slightly different setSampling signature (some older versions differ). The common signature is: bme.setSampling( Adafruit_BME280::MODE_NORMAL, Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X2,   // temp Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X16,  // pressure Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X1,   // humidity Adafruit_BME280::FILTER_X16, Adafruit_BME280::STANDBY_MS_1000 ); If your version doesn’t accept STANDBY_MS_1000, it may be named STANDBY_MS_1000 or STANDBY_MS_1000 (same) but sometimes only has STANDBY_MS_500. Another possibility: older versions omit the standby parameter entirely. Quick fix approach: 1) Comment out setSampling entirely and see if it compiles. Defaults still work. 2) Or adapt to your signature: in Arduino IDE, Ctrl+click setSampling to open the library header and see the exact parameters. If you paste the exact error line plus the line of code you’re calling, I can adjust precisely.

The line is: bme.setSampling(Adafruit_BME280::MODE_FORCED, Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X2, Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X16, Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X1, Adafruit_BME280::FILTER_X16, Adafruit_BME280::STANDBY_MS_1000); It underlines the whole call.

Two changes: - MODE_FORCED is fine, but in forced mode you typically call takeForcedMeasurement() before reading. If you’re not doing that, use MODE_NORMAL. - More importantly, your library likely doesn’t have the 6-argument version (with standby). Try the 5-argument version: bme.setSampling( Adafruit_BME280::MODE_NORMAL, Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X2, Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X16, Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X1, Adafruit_BME280::FILTER_X16 ); If that compiles, great. If it still doesn’t, then your setSampling might accept only (sensor_mode) or something older; in that case, easiest is to remove setSampling and proceed.
The 5-argument version compiles. Now it runs, but my LCD sometimes flickers when SD writes happen. Any way to reduce that?

Common causes are timing and I2C bus noise, but usually it’s just that SD writes take a moment and your LCD update happens to coincide. Ways to reduce flicker: 1) Update LCD less often (e.g., every 500–1000 ms is already okay; but avoid clearing the screen each update). 2) Do “partial updates”: - Don’t call lcd.clear() every refresh. - Overwrite only the characters that changed, or rewrite full lines without clearing. 3) Make sure your LCD strings are always exactly 16 chars (pad with spaces) so leftover characters don’t remain. Also, SD writes can cause supply dips. Add/confirm: - 100–470 µF capacitor at SD VCC/GND (close to module). - Keep SPI wires short. - Common ground solid. If your code currently calls lcd.clear() on every update, that’s the first thing I’d change.
